transcript_v1.text: Hi, welcome to our booth. We are Group 106 and our sponsor is Murphy Oil, an upstream oil and gas company from the U.S.
The goal of our capstone is to perform data analysis on their inspection data and understand what are the main drivers of corrosion on their equipment.
Particularly, we are focused on their horizontal separators and all of the piping circuit connected to it.
Horizontal separators are just big pieces of equipment that separates the produce into oil, gas, and water.
And for it to be further refined and distributed.
To find these drivers of corrosion, we used both manual analysis based on literature information available on corrosion mechanisms that we should be expecting to see.
And we also trained machine learning models on the data.
We fed all of the hundreds of parameters into the model when we were making predicts the percentage for loss of the piping.
Our models have an average accuracy of 86% and an average error of 2.6%.
Meaning that, on average, the rear wall loss will be off by about 3%.
This might seem like a lot, but it's actually pretty solid metrics for this type of problem.
The machine learning approach is really good here because it allows us to just pass the data sets into the models.
And the models itself learns the patterns on the data.
And we then just open and explore the models to surface these relationships on the data.
Instead of having to go through all of the thousands of combinations that can be done on the parameters.
After understanding the correlations, the next step on the project was to validate our findings against historical failure events.
Overall, our macro trends were confirmed, such as the regions and the circuits that are more susceptible to corrosion.
But some of the more specific insights couldn't be validated because of a lack of details on the failure events.
At the end, we then concatenated all of those insights into a Power BI dashboard that lets the Murphy oil engineers explore the correlations and trends.
It gets updated automatically when more inspection data is taken.
And it risk ranks the locations of the equipments that need to be inspected more often or receiving special attention.
Based on a formula that we have created that uses the validated parameters that we've seen.

transcript_vfinal.text: Hi, welcome to our booth. We are Group 106, and we partnered with Murphy Oil, a U.S.-based upstream oil and gas company.
Our capstone looks at their inspection data to answer one practical question: what is really driving corrosion in their equipment?
We focused on horizontal separators and the piping circuits connected to them.
A horizontal separator is a large vessel that takes the production stream and separates it into oil, gas, and water,
so each stream can move on for further refining and distribution.
To identify the corrosion drivers, we started with manual analysis, using the literature to map the mechanisms we would expect to see.
Then we took that same inspection data and trained machine learning models on it.
We fed the models hundreds of variables and asked a simple question: can they predict percent wall loss in the piping?
Across the models, we reached about 86% accuracy, with an average error of 2.6%.
In practical terms, that means the predicted wall-loss percentage is usually within about 3% of the actual value.
That might still sound high, but for a problem with this many interacting factors, those are strong results.
Machine learning helps here because it lets us use the full data set at once, instead of hand-testing every factor.
The models can spot patterns that are hard to see by eye.
Then we open the models back up and examine which relationships matter most,
rather than manually working through thousands of possible parameter combinations, one by one.
Once we had those correlations, we compared them with historical failure events.
At a high level, those failure records supported our main trends, including the regions and circuits most susceptible to corrosion.
But some of the more specific insights could not be confirmed, because the failure records did not include enough detail.
Finally, we brought those insights into a Power BI dashboard that lets Murphy Oil engineers explore the patterns and correlations for themselves.
It updates automatically whenever new inspection data comes in.
It also risk-ranks the equipment locations that may need more frequent inspection or closer attention,
based on a formula we created using the validated parameters we identified.
